coredata.dc:title: Fluorescent thermochromic wood-based composite phase change materials based on aggregation-induced emission carbon dots for visual solar-thermal energy conversion and storage
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Yushan
Yang, Haiyue
Wang, Ying
Ma, Chunhui
Luo, Sha
Wu, Zhenwei
Zhang, Zhanshuo
Li, Wei
Liu, Shouxin
coredata.dc:description: Efficient solar-thermal energy conversion and storage is significant to overcome current energy shortage problems. Monitoring solar-thermal energy storage process by an evident and convenient display is conducive to improving energy utilization. Herein, fluorescent thermochromic wood-based composite phase change materials (WPCMs) were constructed for visual solar-thermal energy conversion and storage. It was fabricated by encapsulating polyethylene glycol (PEG) and aggregation-induced emission carbon dots (AIE-CDs) showing blue dispersed emission and red aggregation-induced emission into delignified wood (DW). The DW well-preserved the distinctive anisotropic porous structure and prevented the leakage problem of PEG. The WPCMs possessed great solar-thermal conversion capacity benefitting from strong and broad solar light absorption behaviors of AIE-CDs. Additionally, WPCMs showed real-time and visual fluorescent thermochromic property, it exhibited red AIE, and the fluorescence (FL) decreased and shifted into the blue emission band under solar radiation. The solar-thermal energy conversion and storage led to solid–liquid transformation of WPCMs, which promoted AIE-CDs’ dispersion, thereby changing the FL. It also exhibited a high latent heat of fusion (160.8 J·g−1), favorable stability over 150 heating–cooling cycles, thermal stability below 210 °C and good shape stability. The WPCMs can be extended to applications in energy-saving buildings and optical lighting materials with visual thermal regulation capability.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Phase change material
Thermochromic
Solar-thermal energy conversion
Thermal energy storage
Carbon dot
Aggregation-induced emission

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The present work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (31890773, 32071719) and the Natural Science Foundation of Heilongjiang Province (YQ2020C016).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic illustration of the AIE-CD preparation. (b) Photographs of the two-switch-mode luminescence principle of the AIE-CDs under sunlight (left) and a UV lamp (365 nm, right). (c) Schematic illustration of FL mechanism of the carbon cores and surface states of the AIE-CDs. TEM (HRTEM, inset) images (d) and size distribution histograms (g) of AIE-CDs. UV–Vis absorption and FL excitation and emission spectra of the AIE-CD ethanol solution (e) and powder (h). FL emission spectra of AIE-CD ethanol solution (f) and powder (i) under different excitation wavelengths.

(a) FL emission spectra of AIE-CD powder solutions in different solvents. (b) FL emission spectra of AIE-CD PEG solution at 25 °C and 70 °C; the inset presents photographs of the AIE-CD PEG solution at different temperatures, irradiated by sunlight and a UV lamp (365 nm), respectively. (c) TEM images of AIE-CD DMF solution with aggregation and dispersion dual states. (d) Size distribution histograms of AIE-CD DMF solution in aggregation.
Schematic of WPCM construction and thermochromic mechanism.

SEM images of cross-sectional (a) DW, (b) PEG/DW, and (c) PEG/DW/CDs; the insets in (a–c) present photographs under sunlight, respectively. (d) FL microscopy images of PEG/DW/CDs under excitation at a wavelength of 500–580 nm. (e) UV–Vis absorption and FL excitation and emission spectra of PEG/DW/CDs. (f) FL emission spectra of PEG/DW/CDs for different excitation wavelengths. (g) FL emission spectra of PEG/DW/CDs with different AIE-CD concentrations under 340 nm excitation. (h) FL emission spectra of PEG/DW/CDs under different temperature and excitation at a wavelength of 340 nm. (i–l) FL microscopy images of PEG/DW/CDs at different temperature and excitation at a wavelength of 300–380 nm for (k) and 500–580 nm for (i, j and l). The scale bars in SEM images and their insets are 100 μm and 1 cm, respectively. The scale bars in FL microscopy images represent 20 μm.

(a) DSC curves of PEG, PEG/DW, and PEG/DW/CDs4. (b) DSC curves of PEG/DW/CDs4 before and after 150 heating–cooling cycles. (c) TGA curves and (d) corresponding DTG curves of AIE-CDs, DW, PEG, PEG/DW, and PEG/DW/CDs4. (e) UV–Vis absorption spectra of PEG/DW/CDs4 from 200 to 2500 nm. (f) Temperature–time curves of PEG/DW/CDs4 and AIE-CDs upon turning on and off the light. (g) IR images of PEG/DW/CDs4 upon turning on and off the light.
Liquid leakage experiment of PEG, PEG/DW, and PEG/DW/CDs4 at 25 °C and 60 °C. The scale bars represent 0.6 cm.

(a) Schematic illustration of using WPCMs as a sunlight-driven fluorescent thermochromic house cover for real-time and visible solar-thermal energy conversion and storage. (b) Temperature-time curves of (c) and (d) test process upon turning on and off the light, the insets present photographs of the WPCMs before and after light irradiation 600 s under UV lamp (365 nm). (c) IR images of NW box exterior and WPCM box exterior upon turning on and off the light. (d) The photograph of test process of temperature–time for NW box interior and WPCM box interior, the scale bars represent 3 cm.
Thermal properties of PEG, PEG/DW, and PEG/DW/CDs.

head.ce:title: Fluorescent thermochromic wood-based composite phase change materials based on aggregation-induced emission carbon dots for visual solar-thermal energy conversion and storage

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Bio-based Material Science & Technology (Northeast Forestry University), Ministry of Education, Harbin 150040, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Bio-based Material Science & Technology (Northeast Forestry University), Ministry of Education

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Bio-based Material Science & Technology (Northeast Forestry University), Ministry of Education, Harbin 150040, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Engineering Research Center of Advanced Wooden Materials, Ministry of Education, Harbin 150040, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Engineering Research Center of Advanced Wooden Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Engineering Research Center of Advanced Wooden Materials, Ministry of Education, Harbin 150040, PR China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this study and share first authorship.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fluorescent thermochromic wood-based composite phase change materials based on aggregation-induced emission carbon dots possessed effective solar-thermal conversion, storage and visual color change under solar radiation, especially as replacements for energy-saving buildings and optical lighting materials with thermal regulation capability.

Efficient solar-thermal energy conversion and storage is significant to overcome current energy shortage problems. Monitoring solar-thermal energy storage process by an evident and convenient display is conducive to improving energy utilization. Herein, fluorescent thermochromic wood-based composite phase change materials (WPCMs) were constructed for visual solar-thermal energy conversion and storage. It was fabricated by encapsulating polyethylene glycol (PEG) and aggregation-induced emission carbon dots (AIE-CDs) showing blue dispersed emission and red aggregation-induced emission into delignified wood (DW). The DW well-preserved the distinctive anisotropic porous structure and prevented the leakage problem of PEG. The WPCMs possessed great solar-thermal conversion capacity benefitting from strong and broad solar light absorption behaviors of AIE-CDs. Additionally, WPCMs showed real-time and visual fluorescent thermochromic property, it exhibited red AIE, and the fluorescence (FL) decreased and shifted into the blue emission band under solar radiation. The solar-thermal energy conversion and storage led to solid–liquid transformation of WPCMs, which promoted AIE-CDs’ dispersion, thereby changing the FL. It also exhibited a high latent heat of fusion (160.8 J·g−1), favorable stability over 150 heating–cooling cycles, thermal stability below 210 °C and good shape stability. The WPCMs can be extended to applications in energy-saving buildings and optical lighting materials with visual thermal regulation capability.

ce:section.ce:para: Solar energy is considered as the most prospective and plenteous renewable energy resource on the earth. The efficient conversion and storage of solar energy are significant for improvement of the energy utilization and the realization of sustainable development[1–4]. Directly converting solar energy into heat is generally more efficient than other solar energy conversion techniques such as photovoltaics (solar–electric conversion) and photosynthesis (solar–chemical conversion)[5–7]. Incorporating photothermal materials in the phase change material system has been shown to be the most effective strategy for solar–thermal conversion.

Phase change materials (PCMs), and particularly organic PCMs, possess enormous superiority for thermal energy storage resulting from their large latent heat and the isothermal property of the storage procedure. In addition, PCMs can absorb excessive thermal energy from circumstance and release it by the phase change process, which could effectively regulate the ambient temperature[8–11]. Nevertheless, the leakage of solid–liquid PCMs in the process of the phase change greatly limits their practical applications. Porous materials, such as mesoporous silica[12], expanded graphite[13], graphene[14], aerogel[15], carbon foam[16], metal organic frameworks[17], and wood[18], are considered the most promising candidates for supporting materials to solve the leakage issue. Among them, wood is an attractive supporting material for PCM encapsulation, as it is an abundant and renewable resource with a unique hierarchical structure, favorable mechanical toughness, and higher absorption capacity after lignin removal.

However, it is impossible to directly realize efficient capture, conversion and storage of solar energy by pure PCMs owing to their extremely weak photo absorption ability. Currently, photothermal materials with effective photon capturing ability, including semiconductor materials[19], plasmonic[20]and magnetic[21] metals, and carbon materials[22], have been introduced to prepare sunlight-driven PCMs. However, most of the above photothermal materials are dark colored or even black, which diminishes the aesthetics. Additionally, it is unable to realize the monitoring of thermal energy capture quantity and storage/release time, as well as solidification/melting point of PCMs, resulting in low energy utilization. Because solar-thermal energy storage will be delayed or even be unfeasible while the ambient temperature did not reach the phase change condition. Thus, it is necessary to design the functional WPCMs with real-time, visual, and on-site monitoring solar-thermal energy storage/release process[23,24]. The thermochromic materials featuring tunable colors with the change of ambient temperature have been doped into PCMs to enable monitoring of the specific temperature during energy storage and release. For example, those based on organic[25], inorganic and liquid crystal thermochromic[26] materials. However, the composition of thermochromic organic compounds is complex and toxic, the discoloration temperature of thermochromic inorganic compounds is too high (nearly 100 °C), and the thermochromic liquid–crystal compounds are expensive. Most importantly, these materials cannot simultaneously realize sunlight absorbance and visible color response towards temperature change.

Benefiting from the excellent sunlight absorbance capacity, low cost, non-toxicity and stability, CDs have been abroad used in photothermal-related conversion systems, such as for oil-spill cleanup [27], phase change energy storage[28], seawater evaporation[29], and desalination[30]. In addition to the above advantages, facile luminescence tunability is a significant advantage of CDs, which shows great potential as a thermochromic indicator[31–36]. Recently, we constructed luminescent transparent wood based on lignin-derived CDs and used it as a self-detecting building material for real-time and visual monitoring of indoor formaldehyde gas levels by color-responsive ratiometric fluorescence (FL) and delayed room temperature phosphorescence dual channels[37]. Additionally, we also encapsulated red/near infrared (NIR) dual-emitting (650/710 nm) lignin-derived CDs into WPCMs to design full-wood photoluminescent and photothermic materials for thermal energy storage. The FL intensity decreased with increasing temperature (NIR-radiated heat) because of the solid–liquid change of the WPCMs[28]. Inspired by the above two studies, the use of WPCMs prepared by encapsulating CDs whose luminescence is visually sensitive to the phase change process and show effective photon capturing ability is a desirable choice for fabricating sunlight-driven fluorescent thermochromic WPCMs.

Herein, we constructed fluorescent thermochromic WPCMs based on aggregation-induced emission (AIE)-CDs for visual solar-thermal energy conversion and storage. The WPCMs possessed great photothermal conversion and fluorescent thermochromic property under solar radiation. And the corresponding mechanism had also been explored. It had a high latent heat of fusion, favorable thermal stability, reversibility, and shape stability.

The AIE-CD preparation method was based on Hu’s and used solvothermal treatment of MA and DTSA in glacial acetic acid (Fig. 1
a)[38]. Under the 365 nm UV irradiation, the AIE-CD ethanol solution emitted blue FL. After adding water, the solution turned into a turbid liquid, indicating its hydrophobicity. In addition, the blue FL faded away and red FL emerged. The AIE-CD powder with red solid-state FL was obtained upon further purification and drying (Fig. 1 b).

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and X-ray diffraction (XRD) were used to characterize the morphologies and structures of the AIE-CDs. As observed in Fig. 1 d and g, the average size of the AIE-CDs was 6.64 ± 0.13 nm with the size distribution in the range of 4.3–9.7 nm. The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images in the insets of Fig. 1 d indicate that the AIE-CDs possessed high crystallinity with a lattice-spacing distance of around 0.21 nm, which was close to that of the (100) plane of graphitic carbon[39,40]. The XRD patterns contained two diffraction peaks at 20.06° (d002 = 0.34 nm) and 43.67° (d100 = 0.21 nm), as observed in Figure S1
a, suggesting that the AIE-CDs consisted of the amorphous carbon phase with partial graphitization[41]. The chemical compositions of the AIE-CDs were performed by Fourier-transform infrared spectra (FT-IR) and X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS). The FT-IR spectra ( Figure S1
b) indicate that the AIE-CDs contained C–H alkyl (3057, 2963, and 2860 cm−1), S–H (2657 cm−1), N–C = O (1670 cm−1), C = C (1458 cm−1), C–N (1407 cm−1), aromatic C–NH (1255 cm−1), C–O (1146 cm−1), C–S (681 cm−1), and S–S (496 cm−1) functional groups. Interestingly, no hydrophilic groups such as hydroxyl or amino groups (3000–3400 cm−1) appeared, resulting in the hydrophobic behaviors of the AIE-CDs. The XPS spectra ( Figure S1
c) of the AIE-CDs contained four typical peaks of C1s (284.8 eV), O1s (532.38 eV), N1s (399.88 eV), and S2p (163.92 eV), and the atomic ratios were calculated to 71.60%, 17.51%, 2.69%, and 8.20%, respectively. The high-resolution C1s spectra ( Figure S1
d) exhibited three peaks at 284.76, 285.25, and 288.93 eV, corresponding to C = C/C–C, C = O/C = N, and C–N, respectively. The high-resolution N1s spectra in Figure S1
e contained two peaks at 399.35 and 400.50 eV, corresponding to pyridinic N and pyrrolic N, respectively. As shown in Figure S1
f, the S2p spectra exhibited three peaks at 163.75, 164.50, and 165.00 eV, assigning to S–S, S–H, and S–C, respectively.

The Ultraviolet–Visible (UV–Vis) absorption, FL emission, and excitation spectra of the AIE-CD solution and AIE-CD powder were tested to evaluate their optical behaviors. Seen fromFig. 1 e and S2a, the UV–Vis absorption spectra of the AIE-CD solution possessed two typical UV-Vis absorption peaks at 310 and 420 nm and a weak peak at 540 nm. The peaks at 310 and 420 nm were corresponded to the π–π* transition of C = C and n–π* of C = O or C–N/S in the carbon core of the AIE-CDs, respectively. The peak at 540 nm corresponds to the n–π* transitions of the surface states containing C = N/O and C–O/S structures of AIE-CDs, and their water solution was more obvious than ethanol solution. The absorption bands of the AIE-CD powder were broader and weaker located at 300–750 nm because of the occurrence of π–π stacking of carbon cores in the AIE-CD powder, demonstrating great potential for absorbing the energy of sunlight (Fig. 1 h) [30]. On the basis of the molecular exciton coupling theory, the absorption redshift of the AIE-CD powder suggests that the AIE-CDs formed head-to-tail J-aggregates [42]. As observed in Fig. 1 f, the AIE-CD ethanol solution possessed a strong excitation-dependent FL emitting peak at 445 nm and a weak excitation-independent FL emitting peak at 605 nm. In addition, the strongest emission peaks appeared at 445 nm with optimal excitation near 380 nm. After adding water to the AIE-CD ethanol solution, the FL emitting peak at 605 nm became stronger than that at 445 nm, and the FL emitting peak at 445 nm simultaneously showed excitation independence ( Figure S2
b). The FL emission of the AIE-CD powder showed a single stable red emission at 625 nm for excitation at different wavelength, as observed in Fig. 1 i. The absolute FL quantum yield for AIE-CD powder was measured to be 5.89% under 560 nm excitation, which though not very high, but is adequate for some applications. And the FL lifetime of the AIE-CD powder was calculated to be 4.77 ns, as shown in Figure S3 .

To further understand the AIE behavior, we dissolved the AIE-CD powder into five different solvents and studied the resulting FL emission spectra. Under the 365 nm UV irradiation, the AIE-CD ethanol and AA solution both emitted blue FL. The AIE-CD PEG solution also emitted blue FL. The AIE-CD water and DMF solution emitted red and orange FL, respectively, as observed in Figure S4 . The FL emission spectra (Fig. 2
a) reveal obvious blue and red dual emission centers in the AIE-CD water, DMF, and PEG solution. The AIE-CD ethanol and AA solution had only one blue emission center (the red emission nearly disappeared). The TEM images (Fig. 2 c) intuitively show the presence of AIE-CD aggregation in the DMF solution around the AIE-CD monomers, with a size distribution of 13.8–45.3 nm (Fig. 2 d). The HRTEM images ( Figure S5 ) reveal the presence of different carbon lattice planes in the aggregated AIE-CDs, indicating that the AIE-CDs assemble with random orientation. Hence, the aggregation resulted in red FL, whereas blue FL comes from the monomers in the dispersion state.

According to the above results, a convincible CD AIE mechanism can be proposed, as shown in Fig. 1 c. In the dispersion state, the carbon core dominated the luminescence of the AIE-CDs, however, the rotatable symmetrical heterocycles round the disulfide bonds were recessive at this time. The AIE-CD solution possessed excitation-dependent blue FL at this moment. In the aggregation state, the graphitized cores went through π–π stacking interplay and further turned off the blue FL via aggregation-induced quenching (ACQ). Furthermore, the axisymmetric heterocycles went through the restriction of intramolecular rotation (RIR) of symmetrical heterocycles on their disulfide bonds axes, similar with other symmetrical molecules with AIE to induce excitation-independent red FL[43–46].

Interestingly, we found that after cooling melted AIE-CD PEG solution to room temperature, the FL changed from blue to obvious red with a liquid–solid phase change, as seen in the inset of Fig. 2 b. PEG is a common organic PCM, and its phase state can be regulated by ambient temperature. Fig. 2 b presents the FL emission spectra of the AIE-CD PEG solution at different temperatures. The red FL increased accompanying by the decreasing of blue FL when melted AIE-CD PEG solution (70 °C) to solidified AIE-CD PEG solution (25 °C), indicating the solidification of PEG might lead to the aggregation of AIE-CDs and thus a red shift of the FL. Inspired by this finding, the current AIE-CDs are a promising fluorescent thermochromic indicator for intuitive monitoring of the specific temperature during energy storage and release.

SEM was used to examine the microstructure of the (natural wood) NW, DW, PEG/DW, and PEG/DW/CDs. We obtained natural basswood samples with thicknesses of 0.3 cm by cutting a tree trunk perpendicular to the tree growth direction. Basswood has higher absorption capacity after lignin removal because of its low density[48]. After delignification, the wood color changed from yellow to white and the UV–Vis absorption band in the range of 430–750 nm also disappeared, as observed in Figure S6 and the inset of Fig. 4
a. After lignin removal, the wood structure was porous, which was beneficial for polymer infiltration, while the aligned microchannels (lumens) were well preserved (Fig. 4 a) [49]. The wood lumens were completely filled with PEG, creating a dense structure without interface separation resulting from the hydrogen-bond interaction between cellulose and PEG in DW (Fig. 4 b). This was beneficial for preventing leakage of PEG. There was no obvious change in the morphology for PEG/DW after adding the AIE-CDs, benefiting from the nano size of the AIE-CDs, as seen in Fig. 4 c. In addition, the surface groups on the AIE-CDs could result in stronger bonding to the DW. The color of the PEG/DW/CDs in the inset of Fig. 4 c, was completely red and the FL microscopy images of PEG/DW/CDs in Fig. 4 d exhibited well-distributed, bright red FL emission both indicated the AIE-CDs were fully filled. Additionally, all the characteristic peaks of PEG, AIE-CDs, and DW appeared in the XRD and FT-IR spectra of PEG/DW/CDs ( Figure S7 and S8 ), proving that the PEG/DW/CDs were successfully constructed. The UV–Vis absorption, FL emission, and excitation spectra of PEG/DW/CDs are presented in Fig. 4 e. In general, the UV–Vis absorption tendency of the PEG/DW/CDs was similar as that of the AIE-CD powder. As observed in Fig. 4 f, the PEG/DW/CDs possess excitation-dependent FL emitting peak for the strongest at 465 nm with optimal excitation near 340 nm and an excitation-independent FL emitting peak at 605 nm. Fig. 4 g presents the FL emission spectra of the PEG/DW/CDs with different concentrations of AIE-CDs excited at 340 nm. The PEG/DW showed an extremely weak FL emission peak at 420 nm, which did not obviously appear in the PEG/DW/CDs FL emission spectra. With increasing concentration of AIE-CDs, the FL emission displayed ratiometric change, with the blue FL gradually decreasing and red FL gradually increasing as a result of the AIE-CD aggregation caused by high concentration, gradually, as observed in Figure S9 . The white-light emission of PEG/DW/CDs2 indicated its feasibility as a planar light source. Interestingly, the FL intensity of the PEG/DW/CDs also displayed ratiometric change at different temperatures, that is, red FL decreasing and blue FL increasing with increasing temperature (Fig. 4 h). Heating triggered the solid–liquid transformation of the WPCMs. The solid–liquid transformation led to changes in the aggregation state of the AIE-CDs, thereby changing the FL emission. The ratiometric FL emission could be used for visual monitoring the specific temperature during energy storage and release by the naked eye from the obvious FL color change since the distance between dual emission bands was more than 100 nm. As shown in Fig. 4 i and 4j, when heating the PEG/DW/CDs to 45 °C, the red FL emission intensity decreased and slight melting of PEG appeared on the material surface. At this time, the porous structure in the wood could still be seen. However, as the temperature continued to rise to 70 °C (Fig. 4 k), the red FL disappeared and the blue FL appeared. The porous structure was difficult to observe because of the complete melting of PEG. After cooling the PEG/DW/CDs to room temperature (25 °C), the red FL was restored and the porous structure reappeared, as observed in Fig. 4 l. This demonstrated that PEG/DW/CDs are desirable fluorescent thermochromic WPCMs with broad application prospects in reversible and preventing leakage.

The thermal properties of the PEG and WPCMs were characterized. Differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) curves of PEG, PEG/DW, and PEG/DW/CDs are presented in Fig. 5
a, and the related values are sum up in Table 1 . Seen from Table 1, the PEG displayed a high fusion enthalpy (177.6 J·g−1), indicating that it is a promising material for thermal energy storage. PEG/DW exhibited a lower fusion enthalpy than that of PEG, since DW was used as a supporting material and not as a working substance. On the one hand, although the fusion enthalpy of the PEG/DW/CDs4 was 3.3 J·g−1 lower than that of PEG/DW, the value still reached 160.8 J·g−1, which was higher than other PEG/DW/CDs. Moreover, the content of PEG in PEG/DW/CDs4 was calculated to be 90.54%, which was also higher than other PEG/DW/CDs, supporting the great thermal energy storage and release ability. Thus the phase change performance of PEG/DW/CDs4 was most optimal. On the other hand, seen from Fig. 4 g, the FL intensity of PEG/DW/CDs1 and PEG/DW/CDs2 at 465 nm was far higher than at 605 nm (even there was on peak at 605 nm for PEG/DW/CDs1), which is not suitable for construction ratiometric sensor for visual monitoring the solar-thermal energy storage process. On this condition, when the temperature rises a little, the peak value at 605 nm may drop to zero quickly, and then no peak value data will be observed on the FL spectrum while keeping heating up. Although, the ratiometric FL emission behaviors of PEG/DW/CDs3 and PEG/DW/CDs4 were similar, through comprehensive consideration of the ratiometric optical and phase change performance, PEG/DW/CDs4 was selected as the optimal sample for subsequent study. After 150 heating–cooling cycles, PEG/DW/CDs4-150 had a similar phase-change temperature as PEG/DW/CDs4 before the cycling treatment, and the enthalpy of fusion was merely decreased by 12.6 J·g−1, as seen in Table 1 and Fig. 5 b. These results indicate that PEG/DW/CDs exhibited long-term stability and service life.

Tm: melting temperature; Tf: freezing temperature; ΔHm: latent heat of fusion; ΔHs: latent heat of solidification; Ƞ (%):mass fraction of PEG loading, which can be calculated using Eq (2): Ƞ (%) = ΔHm (PEG/DW/CDs) / ΔHm (PEG)

It is necessary to explore the thermal stability of WPCMs for practical applications. Fig. 5 c and d present thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) and derivative thermogravimetric (DTG) curves of the AIE-CDs, DW, PEG/DW, and PEG/DW/CDs4. The AIE-CDs possess high thermal stability with a degradation peak at 327 °C, which differs from previous reports as the AIE-CDs were hydrophobic. There was no degradation peak of adsorbed water under 100 °C. Therefore, the current AIE-CDs were more thermally stable and are a great candidate for thermochromic and photothermal materials. DW underwent one-step thermal degradation, starting at around 210 °C and ending at 360 °C, and the maximum weight loss rate appeared at 317 °C. The weight loss of PEG occurred in only one step in the range of 295 °C to 440 °C, assigning to the maximum degradation peak appearing at 400 °C. For PEG/DW and PEG/DW/CDs4, there were two distinct degradation procedure, corresponding to the degradation of DW and PEG. DW showed thermal stability below 210 °C, indicating that the working temperature of PEG/DW/CDs cannot exceed 210 °C in practical production and life.

The UV–Vis-NIR absorption spectrum of PEG/DW/CDs4 is shown in Fig. 5 e, showing strong and broad light absorption across the solar spectrum in the wavelength range of 200–2500 nm, suggesting its great solar–thermal conversion ability. The photothermal properties of PEG/DW/CDs and AIE-CDs were then investigated using a xenon lamp to simulate solar radiation (150 mW·cm−2), and the temperature was test using an infrared thermal (IR) camera. The PEG/DW/CDs4 exhibited regulated characteristic when the light turned on or off, as seen in Fig. 5 f and 5g. Three stages occurred when the light turned on to stimulate photothermal conversion. Firstly, the temperature quickly rose to around 55.7 °C from 33.3 °C in 3.9 min. Secondly, the temperature rose very slowly between 55.7 °C and 58.8 °C in 12.8 min, forming a plateau and indicating the occurrence of solar–thermal energy storage. Finally, when the temperature rose exceed the plateau, the temperature once again rose to 70 °C at a fast rate. After 33.6 min radiation, the light turned off. The cooling process went through three stages similar to the heating process: a first stage of rapid cooling exceed 41.8 °C; a second slower cooling stage in the range of 41.8 °C to 37.8 °C, constituting the thermal energy release process; and a third stage of rapid cooling to room temperature. The photothermal conversion performance of PEG/DW/CDs with different AIE-CD contents was also test under the same light irradiation. Seen from Figure S10 , the phase change process failed to take place for PEG/DW and PEG/DW/CDs1 because that the low AIE-CD content at this time absorbed very little light for thermal conversion which resulting in it not reached the phase change temperature. As for PEG/DW/CDs2 and PEG/DW/CDs3, although, the phase change process took place, their heating rate was slower than PEG/DW/CDs4. Their plateau period for energy storage was also longer about 10 min than PEG/DW/CDs4. And their maximum temperatures were also lower 7.5 °C than PEG/DW/CDs4. All these results suggested that the photothermal conversion performance for PEG/DW/CDs4 was most optimal. Then, the photothermal conversion performance of AIE-CDs was also explored in Fig. 5 f and S11 . The temperature of AIE-CDs rose from 30.4 °C to 76.8 °C in only 3 min and cooled to 31.2 °C in 7.5 min, which was far less than the heating/cooling time of PEG/DW/CDs4, as there was no energy storage/release substance and no plateau period for AIE-CDs. The high photothermal conversion performance also reflected strong and broad solar light absorption ability of AIE-CDs. These results suggest that PEG/DW/CDs shows great potential for efficient solar-thermal energy conversion and storage ability.

The liquid leakage problem of PEG blocked its practical applications. The liquid leakage experiment was performed at 25 °C and 60 °C to confirm the shape stability of PEG, PEG/DW, and PEG/DW/CDs4, as shown in Fig. 6 . When the temperature was exceed the melting temperature of PEG, PEG turned into a liquid. Nevertheless, there was no liquid leakage appeared in PEG/DW and PEG/DW/CDs4. Because that DW was used as the supporting material, with liquid leakage prevented resulting from the capillary effect and surface tension. These findings demonstrated that the PEG/DW/CDs possessed excellent shape stability.

Currently, buildings and maintaining artificial constructions occupy 30%–40% of the energy consumption all over the world, primarily using in cooling, heating, ventilation, and lighting[49–51]. In view of the current situation, researchers are constantly thinking mitigation tactics. The critical of energy savings is to effectively dominate the energy switch between the interior and exterior of buildings. The WPCMs in this study are promising candidates for visual control of the indoor and outdoor temperature difference. Fig. 7
a is an illustration of a house model which showed different colors (blue and red) in practical application at sunny day and cloudy day, respectively. It demonstrates WPCMs used as house cover could show obvious color change at different temperature. For a sunny day, the house cover will capture photons and store solar energy in the form of heat, emitting blue FL. When it was cloudy, the outdoor temperature is low (25 °C), the house cover will release the heat stored on sunny day to regulate the indoor temperature and convert from blue FL to red FL. It was simple to detect the temperature surroundings by distinct color change without a thermograph, and it gave ground the building material more ornamental.

Then we designed wood box (3.0 cm × 3.0 cm × 3.0 cm) for simulating the above house model in Fig. 7 d. The top side of wood box was regard as the house cover and it was changed into NW and WPCMs, named as NW box and WPCM box, respectively. The wood box interior and exterior temperature was used to simulate indoor and outdoor temperature. The temperature of wood box interior and exterior on turning light on and off was obtained from thermometer and IR image, respectively in Fig. 7 c and 7d. Fig. 7 b showed the internal/external temperature change as time progresses of NW box and WPCM box. Seen from Fig. 5 e and S12, the solar absorption property of WPCMs was stronger than NW, nevertheless, the heating rate of WPCM box exterior was slower than NW box exterior upon turning on the light. And after light irradiation for 9.5 min, the temperature of WPCM box exterior and NW box exterior were 56.8 °C and 68.5 °C, respectively. This is because of WPCMs possessed thermal energy storage process, and NW didn't. Thus the WPCMs was more suitable for using as house cover than NW because the building material surface temperature should not be too high, and the WPCMs were desirable heat insulation building materials. The heating rate of WPCM box interior was also slower than NW box interior upon turning on the light and maximum temperature of NW box interior was higher about 3 °C than WPCM box interior. After turning off the light, the temperature of NW box interior and exterior both cooled to room temperature quickly (in 9.2 min and 9.6 min, respectively). Compared with NW box, the temperature of WPCM box interior and exterior both cooled to room temperature were slower (in 19.9 min and 20.6 min, respectively). These phenomenon reflect the great heat preservation performance of WPCMs compared with NW. Moreover, the FL emission also changed from red into blue after light irradiation for 600 s seen from the insets in Fig. 7 b. These results indicated the current WPCMs have a wide application prospect as building materials for real-time, visual, and on-site control of the indoor and outdoor temperature difference.

In summary, fluorescent thermochromic WPCMs were fabricated by using PEG as the PCM, DW as the supporting material, and AIE-CDs as the thermochromic and photothermal materials. The WPCMs could real-time and visually capture, conversion, and storage of solar energy by fluorescent thermochromic and photothermal conversion possess. The DW well-preserved the distinctive anisotropic porous structure and prevented the leakage problem of PEG with good shape stability. The strong and broad solar light absorption behaviors of AIE-CDs enabled WPCMs great solar-thermal conversion capacity. The molecular rotors and/or vibrators in the AIE-CDs facilitated the intramolecular motions upon photoexcitation, thereby boosting the solar-thermal conversion efficiency. The AIE-CDs emitted blue FL in the dispersion state and red AIE in the aggregation state. Based on CDs’ AIE behaviors, the WPCMs showed real-time and visual fluorescent thermochromic property, it exhibited red AIE, and the FL decreased and shifted into the blue emission band under solar radiation. The solar-thermal energy conversion and storage led to solid–liquid transformation of WPCMs, which promoted AIE-CDs’ dispersion, thereby changing the FL. It also exhibited a high latent heat of fusion (160.8 J·g−1), favorable stability over 150 heating–cooling cycles, thermal stability below 210 °C and good shape stability. The WPCMs provides convenience for production and life, especially as a replacement for energy-saving buildings and optical lighting materials with thermal regulation capability. This operational notion can also develop the great potential for energy management with a visible color change and highly efficient energy utilization.

Chemicals and Materials: Balsa wood was obtained from the local works in Harbin, P. R. China. NaClO2, melamine (MA), dithiosalicylic acid (DTSA), and PEG (average Mn = 2000) were purchased from Macklin (Shanghai, P.R. China). Glacial acetic acid, ethanol, and N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF) were purchased from Guangfu (Tianjin, P. R. China). All chemicals were of analytical grade and used as received without further purification.

Synthesis of AIE-CDs: AIE-CDs were synthesized from solvothermal treatment of MA and DTSA in glacial acetic acid. Firstly, 0.1 g of MA and 0.2 g of DTSA were added into 15 mL of glacial acetic acid under the continuous stirring. Secondly, the homogeneous mixture was poured into a poly (p-phenylene)-lined autoclave (50 mL) and heated at 180 °C for 10 h. After cooling to room temperature, 200 mL of boiled deionized (DI) water was added into the crude product to form AIE-CD powder thus removing the residual raw materials and solvent. At last, the resulting turbid liquid were filtered through 0.22 μm filter membranes to obtain purified AIE-CD powder.

Delignification: Wood pieces (3.0 cm × 3.0 cm × 0.3 cm) were soaked in 2 wt% of NaClO2 solution, and pH was adjusted into 4.6 by glacial acetic acid. Then, the mixture was heated at 80 °C for 14 h. Next, the obtained wood samples were boiled in DI water to remove residual chemicals and freeze-dried for 48 h to obtain DW.

Fabrication of WPCMs: AIE-CD powder of different masses (100, 54, 15, 1, and 0 mg) was added into 30 g of melted PEG under continuous stirring at 70 °C for 20 min. Then, the DW was infiltrated into the PEG and AIE-CD mixture solution under vacuum at 70 °C for 20 min; this process was repeated 4 times. PEG/DW/CDs1, PEG/DW/CDs2, PEG/DW/CDs3, PEG/DW/CDs4, and PEG/DW composites, respectively, were thus obtained.

The liquid leakage experiment of WPCMs was displayed as followings: Firstly, all samples were put on filter paper and heat at 60 °C (exceed the melting point of PEG) for 30 min, then wiped off surface-adhesive PEG by filter paper. Secondly, after cooling down to room temperature, the WPCMs were weighed. Finally, repeated above process for 3 times until there is no weight change occurred for WPCMs before and after heating. All sample characterization was performed after the liquid leakage experiment.

Characterization: The morphology and microstructure of materials were characterized by TEM (JEM 2100, JEOL Ltd., Japan) and SEM (Hitachi TM3030, Hitachi., Japan). FL images were recorded using the inverted microscope (DM IL LED, Leica., Germany). XRD was used to test the crystal structure of obtained materials (D8 Advance, Bruker AXS., Germany) using Cu Kα radiation. The FT-IR (Nicolet iS10, Nicolet., USA) and XPS spectra (ESCALAB 250 XI, Thermo, USA) with 1486.6 eV Al Kα radiation were used to display the element composition and surface functional groups of materials. The binding energies were referenced to the C 1 s line at 284.8 eV. UV–Vis absorption spectra of the solution (TU-1950, Beijing Purkinje General., China) and solid (UV-3600, Shimadzu., Japan) samples were obtained using the UV–Vis spectrometer. FL spectra were obtained using the FL spectrophotometer (Agilent Cary Eclipse, Agilent Technologies., USA), equipped with a 150 W xenon excitation source. Absolute FL quantum yield of the AIE-CDs were measured in a calibrated integrating sphere on a FLS1000 instrument (Edinburgh., UK). The FL lifetimes were recorded on an FLSP920 FL spectrophotometer (Edinburgh., UK). The luminescence lifetimes (τ) were calculated using Eq, (1): τavg = Σαiτi 2/Σαiτi. The thermophysics properties of materials were test by DSC (DSC214 Polyma, Netzsch., Germany) and TG (STA449F3 Jupiter, Netzsch., Germany). The scan rate were 2 °C min−1 and 5 °C min−1, respectively, and both tested in nitrogen atmosphere. IR images and temperature changes were recorded using a IR camera (A35, FLIR., USA) and a xenon lamp (PLS-SXE300UV, Perfect Light., China), which simulates solar irradiation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The present work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (31890773, 32071719) and the Natural Science Foundation of Heilongjiang Province (YQ2020C016).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2021.130426.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Scalable aesthetic transparent wood for energy efficient buildings

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R. Y. Mi, C.J. Chen, T. Keplinger, Y. Pei, S. M. He, D. P. Liu, J. G. Li, J. Q. Dai, E. Hitz, B. Yang, I. Burgert, L. B. Hu, Scalable aesthetic transparent wood for energy efficient buildings, Nature Communications. 11 (2020) 3836.
